title: Asking for directions
setting: You're lost looking for a friend's house. You stop a friendly woman on the sidewalk.

options.es: Sí, busco la casa azul cerca del parque.
options.en: Yes, I'm looking for the blue house near the park.
options.feedback: Perfect — you gave a landmark, which is exactly how directions work here.
Honest! But tell her where you're trying to go so she can help.
But you ARE lost — let her help! Pride won't get you there.

steps.speaker: Señora
steps.es: Ah, sí. De aquí, dos cuadras arriba.
steps.en: Ah, yes. From here, two blocks east.

options.feedback: Smart question! 'Arriba' = east (where the sun rises) confuses every newcomer.
Okay — but make sure you know which way 'arriba' is, or you'll go wrong.
Not 'up'! In Nica directions 'arriba' means east, not upward.

steps.speaker: Señora
steps.es: Exacto. Después una cuadra al sur, en la esquina.
steps.en: Exactly. Then one block south, on the corner.

options.es: Dos arriba y una al sur. ¿Verdad?
options.en: Two east and one south. Right?
options.feedback: Repeating directions back is the best way to lock them in. Smart move.
Hope you've got it! Confirming would've been safer.
No shame in that — though most folks here just point and tell.

steps.speaker: Señora
steps.es: Así es. Que Dios te acompañe, muchacho.
steps.en: That's it. May God go with you, young man.

options.feedback: Warm and respectful. 'Señora' shows the right courtesy.
Nice — returning her blessing is a lovely touch.
A bit flat after such a kind send-off, but it works.
